question_text: M09 - Chronic Disease, Geriatric, and High-Risk Patient Management: Which finding pattern best supports classifying a fictional patient as high risk for RN case-management follow-up?
answer_a: Recent hospitalization, worsening symptoms, medication discrepancy, fall history, and limited caregiver support
answer_b: Stable chronic disease, reliable teach-back, resolved transportation plan, and no recent changes
answer_c: One routine preventive appointment with no functional or medication concern
answer_d: Preference for afternoon phone calls with no clinical or access barrier
rationale: Risk stratification is strongest when several clinical, functional, medication, utilization, and support markers cluster together; the other options describe routine preferences or stable status without high-risk indicators.

question_text: A fictional older adult reports dizziness after discharge and has duplicate bottles of an older diuretic plus a new medication list. What is the best RN case-management action?
answer_a: Tell the patient to stop the older bottle immediately and use only the newest list
answer_b: Assess what the patient is actually taking, identify the discrepancy, provide safety teaching within scope, and coordinate provider or pharmacist review
answer_c: Wait until the next scheduled visit because duplicate bottles are common after discharge
answer_d: Document that the caregiver is responsible and close the medication concern
rationale: The RN should not independently change prescriptions, but should assess actual use, recognize safety risk, coordinate authorized review, and document follow-up because dizziness and duplicate diuretics may create immediate harm.

question_text: A fictional patient with neuropathy and mild memory difficulty has fallen twice this month. Which RN case-management response is most complete?
answer_a: Advise the patient to be more careful and reassess at the next routine interval
answer_b: Focus only on ordering a new walker without reviewing contributing factors
answer_c: Assess circumstances, injury, dizziness, medications, cognition, environment, assistive-device use, and need for provider or therapy follow-up
answer_d: Treat falls as expected aging and avoid escalation unless hospitalization occurs
rationale: Repeated falls require assessment of multiple contributors and coordination with appropriate team members; limiting the response to a reminder or device misses medication, cognition, injury, and escalation concerns.

question_text: Which teaching method best fits chronic disease self-management for a high-risk patient with memory and caregiver-support concerns?
answer_a: Provide every available handout and assume the patient will call if confused
answer_b: Use technical disease-specific terms so the education sounds complete
answer_c: Skip patient teaching and teach only the caregiver because the patient is older
answer_d: Review a few priority warning signs in plain language and ask the patient or caregiver to explain the action plan back
rationale: Teach-back confirms whether the explanation can be used at home and helps the RN adapt education for cognition, literacy, caregiver availability, and condition-specific warning signs.

question_text: Why is caregiver strain a case-management risk marker in high-risk chronic disease planning?
answer_a: It can affect medication follow-through, appointment attendance, monitoring, safety, and transition success
answer_b: It proves the patient cannot participate in the care plan
answer_c: It removes the need for team communication because the caregiver is present
answer_d: It is only a social issue and should not be documented by the RN case manager
rationale: Caregiver strain can directly affect adherence, safety monitoring, transportation, teaching reinforcement, and escalation reliability, so it belongs in the care coordination assessment and plan.

question_text: A fictional patient agrees with all teaching but cannot explain when to call the provider or how medications are organized. What should the RN case manager do next?
answer_a: Record that teaching was completed because the patient nodded during the discussion
answer_b: Re-teach using simpler language, involve approved caregiver support when appropriate, verify teach-back again, and document remaining barriers
answer_c: Assume the patient is refusing care and end the education attempt
answer_d: Diagnose dementia and remove the patient from decision-making
rationale: Poor teach-back indicates the teaching plan did not yet work; the RN should adapt education, include support within consent and policy, document barriers, and coordinate follow-up without independently diagnosing cognition.

question_text: A patient has diabetes, heart failure, kidney disease, poor intake, dizziness, and missed glucose checks. Which priority judgment is most appropriate?
answer_a: Address only diabetes education because glucose checks were missed
answer_b: Schedule routine follow-up after the next provider visit without additional assessment
answer_c: Prioritize the combined risk cluster and coordinate medication, symptom, nutrition, monitoring, and provider communication follow-up
answer_d: Close the case because multiple chronic conditions are outside RN case-management scope
rationale: Multiple interacting conditions can magnify risk; the RN case manager should organize the cluster into coordinated assessment, teaching, communication, and follow-up rather than treating one item in isolation.

question_text: Which finding should prompt the RN case manager to strengthen the monitoring and coordination plan for a high-risk chronic disease patient?
answer_b: The patient owns a scale and glucose meter
answer_c: The caregiver knows the clinic phone number
answer_d: The patient is eating poorly and missing ordered self-monitoring checks after a recent medication change
rationale: Poor intake plus missed monitoring after medication changes can increase risk for decompensation, hypoglycemia, dehydration, or medication-related harm and should influence follow-up intensity.

question_text: A fictional patient reports dizziness, two recent falls, medication confusion, missed follow-up, and caregiver exhaustion. Which follow-up timing is best supported?
answer_a: Same-day assessment and communication through appropriate provider, pharmacy, supervisory, or team pathways according to policy
answer_b: Routine monthly call because each issue can be handled separately
answer_c: No follow-up until the caregiver requests help
answer_d: Annual review because the patient is already enrolled in case management
rationale: The risk cluster suggests potential current safety threats and unresolved medication or fall concerns, so same-day follow-up and communication are more appropriate than routine monitoring.

question_text: Which provider communication is most useful for M09 high-risk RN case management?
answer_a: The patient is complicated and needs something done soon
answer_b: Fictional patient reports dizziness on standing, two falls this month, duplicate diuretic bottles, poor intake, and missed glucose checks; request medication and symptom guidance today
answer_c: The caregiver seems stressed, but there is no need to include clinical details
answer_d: Please approve all requested services and guarantee coverage
rationale: Effective provider communication is concise, factual, patient-specific, time-sensitive, and clear about the requested action; vague or payer-promising language does not support safe coordination.

question_text: Which care-plan goal is most measurable and patient-centered for a fictional high-risk chronic disease case?
answer_a: Patient will improve chronic disease management
answer_b: Caregiver will handle all medications without further RN involvement
answer_c: Patient or approved caregiver will explain three warning signs, identify who to call, and confirm the next follow-up date before the end of the call
answer_d: RN will make the patient compliant with the care plan
rationale: The correct goal is observable, tied to teach-back, includes the call pathway and follow-up date, and avoids vague improvement language or coercive compliance wording.

question_text: Which documentation element best shows evaluation of a high-risk care plan?
answer_a: High risk noted; continue to monitor
answer_b: Patient educated; no further detail needed
answer_d: Teach-back improved after re-education, pharmacist review request sent, provider response pending, and next RN follow-up scheduled for tomorrow
rationale: Evaluation requires evidence of patient or caregiver response, team communication, unresolved items, and a specific next follow-up time; vague monitoring language does not show whether the plan is working.

question_text: How should the M09 module status be represented in learner-facing materials before provider metadata is available?
answer_a: Draft / Pending BRN CEP Approval, with no approval claim and no certificate release until required gates are satisfied
answer_b: BRN-approved because the module contains nursing content
answer_c: Approved after the learner passes the M09 quiz
answer_d: Certificate-ready as soon as the assignment is submitted
rationale: The course-content package remains draft pending approval; learner materials must not claim approval or release certificates before provider metadata and completion evidence gates are satisfied.

question_text: Which statement correctly reflects the measurement approach for M09?
answer_a: The module should be labeled primarily by college-unit terminology
answer_b: M09 targets 3 contact hours supported by Moodle reading, interaction, documentation, quiz, reflection, and completion evidence
answer_c: Credit is based only on the number of quiz questions answered correctly
answer_d: The module should use whichever credit label appears in older templates
rationale: M09 is measured primarily in contact hours, and the 3 contact-hour target is supported by a set of tracked learning activities rather than quiz score alone or stale labels.

question_text: A learner has viewed the lesson pages and passed the quiz but has not submitted the documentation exercise or attestation. What is the correct completion decision?
answer_a: Mark M09 complete because the quiz is the only evidence needed
answer_b: Release the certificate because most module work is finished
answer_c: Do not mark complete until required M09 submissions, interaction evidence, quiz status, attestation, and completion gate requirements are satisfied
answer_d: Mark complete if the learner states verbally that the missing work was done
rationale: Completion requires the full Moodle evidence set, not only page views or quiz performance; certificate release also remains blocked until provider approval metadata and all required evidence gates are satisfied.
